![Vanderplas, J. Python Data Science Handbook, O'Reilly 
The contents are available free online at ﷟HYPERLINK "https://jakevdp.github.io/PythonDataScienceHandbook"github.io. 
Main
Simple linear Regression
Relationship between independent variables and dependent variable. Mainly for predicting continuous variables. 
But logistic regression is used for classification tasks.
Simple is line of best fit 
Minimise the leas squares difference
https://www.varsitytutors.com/hotmath/hotmath_help/topics/line-of-best-fit
Multiple linear regression
Linear relationship between several independent variables and dependent variables.
Methods
Ordinary least squares
Maximum likelihood estimation
Ordinary least squares
N 
E(w) = 1/N 
[yi- f(xi,w)]2 
di=1 
Equation 1: Error function. 
Where: 
. xi and yi are values of variables x and y of observations i. 
. N is the number of the observations. 
. w is the vector of coefficients used to define the linear regression. 
. E(w) represents the error based on the weights - we want to minimise this. 
The above function is called error function, objective function, or cost function. 
The aim is to minimise it. 
f(xi,w) 
The function which you see inside the above equation is called the linear 
regression function, or hypothesis function, and is defined as: 
f(xi,w)=Wo+W1X](data:image/png;base64,)  
![N 
E(w) = 1/N 
[yi- f(xi,w)]2 
di=1 
Equation 1: Error function. 
Where: 
. xi and yi are values of variables x and y of observations i. 
. N is the number of the observations. 
. w is the vector of coefficients used to define the linear regression. 
. E(w) represents the error based on the weights - we want to minimise this. 
The above function is called error function, objective function, or cost function. 
The aim is to minimise it. 
f(xi,w) 
The function which you see inside the above equation is called the linear 
regression function, or hypothesis function, and is defined as: 
f(xi,w)=Wo+W1X  
https://www.data-automaton.com/2017/12/25/linear-regression/
After differentiating equation 1 in terms of both w0 and w1 and equating the 
derivative functions to zero, you end up with two equations as follows: 
N 
-2/N 
>.yi-(Wo + W1xi) = 0 
k=0 
Equation 3 
N 
-2/N 
> xi[yi - (wo + w1xi)] = 0 
k=0 
Equation 4  
This can be solved through the closed form or gradient descent.
Closed form uses matrix mathematics and is deterministic.
Gradient descent is trail and error and can get stuck in local minima if the training rate is too low..
The N fraction can be removed and equations 3 and 4 can be reordered to form 
the equations shown below.

Equation 7  
This follows the pattern: 
B=w A 
Equation 8 
To compute the vector w, the expression becomes: 
w=A-1B 
Equation 9  
The function can be expressed as: 
f(xi,w) = Woxio+ W1xi1 + W2xi2 + ..... +Wmxİm 
Equation 10 
This can be generalised to: 
(xi,w) = > wj xji 
Equation 11 
Where: 
· xji is the independent variable xj at observation i. 
. M is the number of independent variables. 
. w is the vector of coefficients.  
x0 is normally set to 1 and this will be the intercept or bias term Wo. The 
generalised form of the differentiation equation for j= 0 to M is: 
SE(w)

The above is stating that the differentiation is for all j (i.e, for all x variables), x 
times (y - y ) where y will be yielded by the hypothesised function on x with 
weights w, f(xi, w) substituting equation 11 into equation 12 gives: 
SE(w)

To apply the closed form approach, a matrix is created for X and another for Y.

https://www.data-automaton.com/2017/12/25/linear-regression/
Multiple regression - multiple independent variables, one dependent variables
Multivariate regression - multiple dependent variables
Closed form works well, but can require lots of resource for large datasets. So if it's too large you might have to choose gradient descent. 
Algorithm1 GradientDescent(E(w), w, n) 
1. Input: 
2. E(w): cost function 
3. w: initial values of coefficients vector 
4. n: learning rate 
5. Output: 
6. w: updated values of coefficients vector 
7. Procedure: 
8. repeat 
A. Wp = Wp-1-ow, , VWp Ew 
9. until VE(w) = 0 
10. return w 
Coefficients (Wp) in the gradient descent algorithm are updated as follows: 
-2 
N 
Wo = won N 
y(i)- (wo + W1x(i)) 
(18) 
i=1 
-2 
N 
W1 = w1-n- 
>[y()-(wo + W1x(i))]x(i) 
(19) 
i=1](data:image/png;base64,)  
![f(x(1), w) = wox") + w1x") + w2x2 + ... + WMX M 
(20) 
or in more compact form: 
f(x(1), w) = > w x(!) 
(21) 
j=0 
Notations: 
x!) is the value of independent variable x; at observation i 
M is number of independent variables (i.e. predictors) 
w is vector of coefficients to identify linear regression function 
5.1 Computing Coefficients Vector w 
In order to compute w, we re-formulate equations (7) and (10) in one equation as follows: 
JE(w) 
w) = ~ {x"[y()-f(x(1), w)], 
àwj 
N 
i=1 
(22) 
Vj € [0 : M], x() = 1 
As an example, suppose we have 2 independent variables (i.e. M = 2). This means we need to compute 3 coefficients 
Wo, W1 and W2. Our regression (i.e. hypothesis) function would be: 
f(x(1), w) = wox!") + w1x(1) + w2x(1) 
(23) 
by setting x(1) = 1, the equation will be: 
f(x(1), w) = wo + w1x(2) + w2x(1) 
(24) 
The corresponded error function derivatives will be: 
DE(W) =~ 2x"[y(1)-(wo +w1x(")+ w2x!)] 
(25) 
i=1  
5.2 Closed Form Approach 
In order to apply the closed-form approach on multivariate linear regression, we build two matrices: the following 
N x (M + 1) matrix X represents the matrix of independent variables. please note that the first column set to 1's is 
corresponded to xa) is equation (22).

y(1)

... 
y(N) 
After building these two matrices, the vector of coefficients w is calculated as: 
w = (XTX)-1xTY 
(26) 
5.3 Gradient Descent Algorithm 
The gradient descent algorithm for 1 independent variable is still valid for multi independent variables. The sole change 
will be in definitions of error function derivatives according to equation (22). As an example, the following updates are 
valid in the case of 2 independent variables: 
WO WO- N

5.3 Gradient Descent Algorithm 
The gradient descent algorithm for 1 independent variable is still valid for multi independent variables. The sole change 
will be in definitions of error function derivatives according to equation (22). As an example, the following updates are 
valid in the case of 2 independent variables:

Please note that in order to compute each coefficient Wj, you need the previous values for all other coefficients, 
including W j .  
Gradient descent didn't line up exactly with the closed form, even when changing learning rate and iterations. Also tried scikitlearn functions, and they still didn't align exactly.
Maximum likelihood estimation
MLE to estimate parameters of probability distribution that best characterise dataset.
A normal or gaussian distribution is assumed. 
The linear function can be modelled as: 
Y = f(X, w) + € 
Equation 15 
Where w is the weight vector and e is the noise or variance.  
Noise with zero mean
E =N (0, 02)  
L(YIX, w, 02) = N(Y| f(X,w), 02)  
Likelihood is Y given X with weights and variance, equals the normal distribution Y generated by function on X and weights and variance
With a predictor variable X ={x1, ............ xn) and a corresponding target variable Y 
={y1, .......... yn), the likelihood function is defined as follows: 
L(YIX, w, 02) = IN(yilw™ xi, o2)](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Likelihood equals multiplying the probabilities of each y observation generated by the corresponding weight x observation, and given the variance
n 
logL(Y|X, w,o2) = 
logN (yi|xi, o2) 
L 
Ji=1  
Xi includes the weights from above so xi=w^T xi
Probability of single point x is given by a gaussian distribution
Ρ (x; μ, σ) = 1/σν 2π exp (-(x - μ) 2 / 202)  
log L (Y|X, w, o2) = N/2 logn - N logo - 1/2 02 
11. (vi - w™xi)2 
li=1 
Equation 21 
From here the regression parameters x and o2 can be found by differentiating 
the function with respect to w and o2. 
In the end, when put in matrix form, the equation boils down to: 
W = (XTx)-1xTY 
Equation 22  
This is the same formula as from the least squares method
OLS is used lots of analytical formulas.
MLE is often used where there is a likelihood function.
Which is a probabilistic method – MLE or OLS?
MLE
Does MLE assume normality of errors?
In linear regression you do, but you can choose any distribution you want.
Which can be used for more than just linear regression, for example logistic regression? MLE or OLS?](data:image/png;base64,)  
![MLE
If you were going to visualise OLS, what would you show? Draw this or sketch it on Paint.
If you were going to visualise MLE, what would you show? Draw this or sketch it on Paint.
MLE regression line fitting height to weight, with observed points and uncertainty band. 
When might you prefer MLE over OLS?
MLE when non-normal. 
MLE for when there is a likelihood function to be used.
OLS for analytical situations
Bias variance trade-off
Ink Drawings
Ink Drawings
Ink Drawings
Ink Drawings
Ink Drawings
Ink Drawings
Ink Drawings
Ink Drawings
Ink Drawings
Ink Drawings](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Bias variance trade-off
Three plots of the same data with values over time as described in the preceding text. 
Underfitted models have high bias and low variance. 
Overfitted models have low bias and high variance. 
Graph showing the relationship of bias and variance as described in the preceding text. 
Bias is the difference between the average prediction and the correct value.
Variance is the amount by which the prediction would change when using a different training dataset.
Diagram showing the effect of variance and bias as described in the preceding text.](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Diagram showing the effect of variance and bias as described in the preceding text. 
Underfitting can be solved by 
finding a more suitable mode,
More data
Hyperparameter tuning
Overfitting can be solved by
Ensemble approach, using data from multiple models, 
Simplifying data(dimensionality reduction)
Limiting the number of learning iterations
Regularisation - penalises complexity](data:image/png;base64,)  

### Ridge regression is a regularisation approach used to correct overfitting. Known as L2 regularisation.

Overfitting is combatted by adding a penalty to the cost/loss function.

Particularly targets multicollinearity, where there is a high correlation between independent variables.

The penalty term regulates the size of the coefficients(weights) and is proportional to the square of the coefficients. The coefficients shrink towards zero as the penalty term increases. This lowers the model's variance.

High coefficient values can be a sign of overfitting. Multicollinearity can cause inappropriately high coefficient estimates.

Residual sum of the squares(RSS) is used to calculate the loss

![n 
(Yi - Y) 
2 
RSS 
= 
i=1](data:image/png;base64,)

![Where Yi is the actual Y value and Y is the predicted Y value.](data:image/png;base64,)

![L2 = ||C||< = C2 + C2 + ··· C2](data:image/png;base64,)

![(Yi-Y)2 + 2 ="_ 
RSS 
= 
Ci 
i=1 
j-1](data:image/png;base64,)

## L1 Least absolute shrinkage and selection operator(LASSO) regression

Known as L1 regularisation.

Unlike L2, it can reduce certain coefficients to zero. Essentially makes it a simpler model.

Penalty is the sum of absolute values of coefficients and multiplied by a lambda.

![The generic supervised loss with L1 looks like this: 
n 
[(w) = Loss(w) + 1 = |wil 
i=1 
Where: 
. Loss (w) is whatever you're optimising (MSE, cross-entropy, hinge, etc.) 
· À is the regularisation strength 
· Wi are the model parameters](data:image/png;base64,)

L1 pushes weights towards exactly zero, so works really well in sparse models. And it becomes more interpretable.

![Why regularisation hits the weights 
L1: 
1Dlwil 
L2: 
2 
T 
You're penalising large values of individual parameters Wi. 
Smaller weights -> smoother, simpler model -> less overfitting.](data:image/png;base64,)

# Logistic regression

Often used for binary classification. It can also be used for multinomial classification and ordinal regression.

Multinomial logistic regression is where there are more than two classes and output is nominal.

Ordinal logistic regression more than two ordinal classes.

![S(x) = 1/(1+e-x)](data:image/png;base64,)

![Graph comparing the linear and logistic models. The linear module produces a straight line.](data:image/png;base64,)

Likelihood

![L(; ξ)](data:image/png;base64,)

Probability of observing e, when data is extracted from probability distribution with parameter set sigma.

![f(xi ; [, 02 ) = (2002 )-1/2 exp (-(xi - u )2/ 202 ) 
Equation 27 
The joint probability density of the sample & is: 
f ( 8; 0 ) = mNi = 1 f (xi; u, o2)](data:image/png;base64,)

![what- arg max L (θ; ξ)](data:image/png;base64,)

# Neural networks

More than one hidden layer is a deep neural network

![Diagram of an artificial neuron as described in the preceding text.](data:image/png;base64,)

Sigmoid activation function has outputs between 0 and 1.

Tanh -1 to 1

ReLu(Rectified linear unit) 0 and infinity.

A hyperparameter called the learning rate determines the size of the weight adjustments.

Gradient descent.

![Graph of gradient descent as described in the preceding text.](data:image/png;base64,)

Sklearn has a multi-layer perceptron(MLP) model, which is a simple neural network with just one layer.

Deep learning can be done with Tensorflow.

Bibliography

- [Brooks-Bartlett, J. (2018), "Probability concepts explained: Maximum likelihood estimation" | Towards Data Science](https://towardsdatascience.com/probability-concepts-explained-maximum-likelihood-estimation-c7b4342fdbb1#:~:text=The%20probability%20density%20of%20observing,parameters%20of%20the%20probability%20distribution.) 
- [El-Gamil (2017a) "Linear Regression", Data Automation](https://www.data-automaton.com/2017/12/25/linear-regression/) (accessed 20-09-24) 
- [El-Gamil (2017b) "Maximum Likelihood for Linear Regression", Data Automation](https://www.data-automaton.com/2021/02/10/maximum-likelihood-for-linear-regression/) (accessed 20-09-24) 
- [GeeksforGeeks (2024) “Bias and Variance in Machine Learning”, GeeksforGeeks, September 20](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/bias-vs-variance-in-machine-learning/) (accessed 30-09-24) 
- [Goel, M. (2020) “The Bias-Variance Tradeoff: A Mathematical View”, SNU_AI](https://medium.com/snu-ai/the-bias-variance-trade-off-a-mathematical-view-14ff9dfe5a3c) (accessed 30-09-24) 
- [Grace-Martin, K. (n.d.) “Confusing Statistical Term: multiple regression and multivariate regression model”, The Analysis Factor](https://www.theanalysisfactor.com/multiple-regression-model-univariate-or-multivariate-glm/) (Accessed:30-09-24) 
- [Murel, J. and Kavlakoglu, E. (2003) "What is ridge regression?, IBM](https://www.ibm.com/topics/ridge-regression)(accessed 23-09-24) 
- [Piduguralla, S. (2023) "Understanding the Sigmoid Function in Logistic Regression: Mapping Inputs to Probabilities" LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/understanding-sigmoid-function-logistic-regression-piduguralla/) (accessed 20-09-24) 
- [Ryan, L. (n.d.) “Maximum Likelihood Estimation versus Least Square Estimation: Understanding two Giants in Statistics”, LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/maximum-likelihood-estimation-versus-least-square-two-ryan-liang-fqwxe/) (accessed 30-09-2024) 
- [Singh, S. (2018) “Understanding the Bias-Variance Tradeoff”, Towards Data Science”, May 21](https://towardsdatascience.com/understanding-the-bias-variance-tradeoff-165e6942b229) (accessed 30-09-2024) 
- [Taboga, M. (2021). "Log-likelihood", Lectures on probability theory and mathematical statistics. Kindle Direct Publishing. Online appendix](https://www.statlect.com/glossary/log-likelihood)(accessed 20-09-24) 
- [Varsity Tutors (n.d.) "Line of Best Fit". Hotmath Topics](https://www.varsitytutors.com/hotmath/hotmath_help/topics/line-of-best-fit) (accessed 20-09-24)
